content.text: An Azure Policy definition is a JSON document that describes a governance rule: what condition to check on a resource, and what effect to apply when that condition is true. Policy definitions are the atomic unit of Azure Policy — they are authored once and can be assigned to any number of scopes independently.

Every policy definition has two logical parts: the condition (the `if` block in `policyRule`) that identifies which resources the policy targets, and the effect (the `then` block) that determines what happens when a resource matches the condition. A resource that does not match the condition is not evaluated by that policy.

attrs.alt: Azure Policy definition structure: mode, parameters, policyRule if/condition, policyRule then/effect

content.text: The `mode` field determines which resource types the policy evaluates. `All` evaluates every resource type including resource groups and subscriptions. `Indexed` evaluates only resource types that support tags and location — it skips types like `Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/subnets` that cannot have tags.

Use `All` when the condition involves resource type, resource group properties, or subscription properties. Use `Indexed` only for tag-based policies where you want to avoid false positives on resource types that cannot carry tags.

Parameters make a policy definition reusable. Instead of hardcoding a tag name, region list, or SKU list in the policy rule, you declare parameters and reference them in the condition and effect. Parameters are typed — `String`, `Array`, `Object`, `Boolean`, or `Integer` — and can have `allowedValues` and `defaultValue` constraints.

The `policyRule` object contains `if` (the condition) and `then` (the effect). The `if` block is a logical expression that returns true or false for each evaluated resource. Operators include `allOf` (AND), `anyOf` (OR), and `not` (NOT). Field references use `field()` to access resource properties.
Deny-effect: require a tag on all resources
Audit-effect: flag VMs missing a specific SKU

Azure provides over 1,500 built-in policy definitions managed by Microsoft. They cover common governance scenarios — allowed locations, required tags, approved VM SKUs, diagnostic settings, TLS versions, public access restrictions. Built-in definitions have `policyType: BuiltIn` and cannot be modified, only assigned with different parameter values.

Custom policy definitions have `policyType: Custom` and are authored by the tenant. They are necessary when a built-in definition does not exist for the specific condition you need to enforce, or when you need a different effect than what the built-in provides.

# Browse built-in policies
az policy definition list \
  --query '[?policyType==`BuiltIn`] | [0:20].{Name:displayName,ID:name}' \
  -o table

# Search built-in policies by keyword
az policy definition list \
  --query '[?policyType==`BuiltIn` && contains(displayName,`storage`)].{Name:displayName,ID:name}' \
  -o table

# Get the full definition of a built-in policy to understand its structure
az policy definition show \
  --name '4fa4b6c0-31ca-4c0d-b10d-24b96f62a751' \
  --query '{Mode:mode,Rule:policyRule,Params:parameters}' \
  -o json

# Create a custom policy definition
az policy definition create \
  --name 'require-cost-center-tag' \
  --display-name 'Require cost-center tag on resource groups' \
  --mode All \
  --rules '@cost-center-tag-policy.json' \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111

# Or create at management group scope (available to all child subscriptions)
az policy definition create \
  --name 'require-cost-center-tag' \
  --display-name 'Require cost-center tag on resource groups' \
  --mode All \
  --rules '@cost-center-tag-policy.json' \
  --management-group mg-corp

Create custom policy definitions at management group scope when they will be assigned at MG level. A definition created at subscription scope can only be assigned within that subscription. A definition at MG scope can be assigned at the MG, any child MG, or any subscription beneath it.
The `if` block supports a rich set of condition operators. Understanding them is essential for writing precise conditions that match exactly the resources you intend.

Terraform's `azurerm_policy_definition` resource manages custom policy definitions. Storing the policy JSON rule in a separate file and loading it with `file()` keeps the Terraform configuration readable.

Before assigning a custom policy definition at a production scope, test its condition against existing resources. The `--what-if` flag for policy is not directly available in the CLI, but you can test by temporarily assigning the definition in `audit` or `DoNotEnforce` mode and checking the compliance state.

# Step 1: Create the policy definition
POLICY_ID=$(az policy definition create \
  --name 'test-deny-public-storage' \
  --mode Indexed \
  --rules '@deny-public-storage.json' \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Step 2: Assign in audit mode to a test subscription
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'test-deny-pub-storage' \
  --policy $POLICY_ID \
  --scope '/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111' \
  --enforcement-mode DoNotEnforce \
  --params '{"effect":{"value":"Audit"}}'

# Step 3: Wait for policy evaluation
echo 'Waiting 90s for policy scan...'
sleep 90

# Step 4: Check which resources would be affected
az policy state list \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'test-deny-pub-storage' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Type:resourceType}' \
  -o table

# Step 5: Clean up the test assignment
az policy assignment delete \
  --name 'test-deny-pub-storage' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'
Checking nested array fields with count

Many Azure resource properties are arrays — NSG security rules, disk attachments, subnet configurations. The `count` expression iterates over array elements and counts how many satisfy a condition. This enables policies like 'deny if any NSG rule allows inbound port 22 from the internet'.

With `mode: All`, a policy can check properties of the resource group containing the evaluated resource using the `resourceGroup()` function. This enables patterns like 'require a cost-center tag on the resource if its resource group has a specific tag'.

A DeployIfNotExists (DINE) policy definition is more complex than deny or audit — it includes a deployment template that Azure Policy executes when a resource is found without the required companion resource. The definition must specify the type of the companion resource, an existence condition to check, the role the policy's managed identity needs, and the ARM deployment template to run.

DINE policy definitions require a managed identity on the assignment with sufficient RBAC to execute the deployment. The `roleDefinitionIds` array specifies which roles the managed identity needs. When assigning a DINE policy, pass `--mi-system-assigned` to az policy assignment create and then grant that identity the required roles.

Custom policy definitions should be versioned like code. The `metadata.version` field is a convention (not enforced by Azure) that tracks the definition version. When making breaking changes — like adding a new required parameter or changing the effect from audit to deny — increment the version and coordinate the change with a new assignment rather than updating the existing assignment in place.

# Update a policy definition (replaces the entire definition)
az policy definition update \
  --name 'require-cost-center-tag' \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --rules '@cost-center-tag-v2.json' \
  --metadata '{"version":"2.0.0","category":"Tags"}'

# Updating a definition updates all existing assignments that reference it
# The change takes effect at the next policy evaluation cycle (up to 24h)
# For immediate re-evaluation, trigger a compliance scan:
az policy state trigger-scan \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111

# List all custom policy definitions in a management group
az policy definition list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query '[?policyType==`Custom`].{Name:name,Display:displayName,Version:metadata.version}' \
  -o table

# Delete a policy definition (must remove all assignments first)
# Check for existing assignments
az policy assignment list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query "[?policyDefinitionId contains 'require-cost-center-tag'].{Name:name,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# Only delete after all assignments are removed
az policy definition delete \
  --name 'require-cost-center-tag' \
  --management-group mg-corp

Azure Policy definitions are JSON documents that pair a condition (`if`) with an effect (`then`). The condition uses field operators to identify which resources the policy targets; the effect determines whether matching resources are denied, audited, modified, or remediated. Parameterised definitions can be reused across scopes with different values at assignment time. Built-in definitions cover most common scenarios; custom definitions are authored at management group scope when built-ins are insufficient. Before assigning any custom definition at a deny effect, test it in DoNotEnforce or audit mode to identify affected resources and avoid unexpected deployment failures.
